coredata.dc:title: Near-infrared fluorescent carbon dots encapsulated liposomes as multifunctional nano-carrier and tracer of the anticancer agent cinobufagin in vivo and in vitro
coredata.dc:creator: Ren, Wei
Chen, Shiqing
Liao, Yuyang
Li, Shumu
Ge, Jiechao
Tao, Fengyun
Huo, Qing
Zhang, Yangyang
Zhao, Zhenwen
coredata.dc:description: Integrating the optical properties of near-infrared fluorescent carbon dots into liposomes may construct a multifunctional nano-system with the potential as a drug carrier, tracer and efficacy intensifier of the anticancer agent. In this study, the liposomes loaded with hydrophilic near-infrared carbon dots as a nano-carrier and tracer of lipophilic anticancer agent cinobufagin were developed. Prepared liposomes were characterized by particle size, morphology and entrapment efficiency. The drug release behavior, the tracer function, the anticancer effect and the side effect were investigated in vitro and in vivo. It was observed that the photoluminescence emission of carbon dots could be strongly enhanced up to 5 times by nano-liposomes. Due to this property, the bio-imaging of CDs + CB liposomes in vitro and in vivo could be clearly obtained. Our results also showed that the CDs + CB liposomes could be uptaken by cells (the lysosomes targeted) and delivered to the tumor site, and undoubtedly, the CDs + CB liposomes demonstrated sustained drug release, enhanced anticancer efficacy and low side effects in vivo. With the assistance of imaging function of CDs, the CDs + CB liposomes can easily display the distribution of drugs, which is very helpful for drug development and may open a novel avenue for drug delivery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Cinobufagin
Multifunctional liposomes
Photoluminescence enhancement
Bio-imaging
Drug delivery

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of CB and CDs entrapment efficiency (EE)
Intracellular uptake of CB and CDs
In vivo imaging and biodistribution of CDs + CB liposomes
In vivo study of the therapeutic efficacy
The preparation and characterization of CDs liposomes
The preparation and characterization of CDs + CB liposomes
Intracellular uptake and in vitro cytotoxicity of CDs + CB liposomes
Bio-imaging of CDs liposomes as tracer of CB in vivo
The performance of anti-tumor activity of CDs + CB liposomes in vivo

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 21575146 , 21874142 , 21635008 , 21621062 and 51472252 ].

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Cyro-TEM images of blank liposomes and (b) CDs liposomes (CDs: 10 μg mL−1). The magnification of the Cyro-TEM images was 20,000.

(a) Photographs of CDs (1) and CDs liposomes (2) under daylight and UV illumination; (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs, blank liposomes, CDs liposomes and CDs + CB liposomes; (c) PL spectra of CDs, blank liposomes, CDs liposomes and CDs + CB liposomes at excitation of 500 nm; (d) Stability of the PL intensity of CDs + CB liposomes in one month.
(a) Time-resolved PL lifetime decay profile of CDs (10 μg mL−1) and (b) CDs liposomes (CDs: 10 μg mL−1) in PBS solution with excitation at 500 nm.

(a) Total ion chromatorgraphy of CB; (b) standard curve of CB established by UPLC-Qtrap-MS; (c) EE of CB in liposomes; (d) in vitro cumulative CB release from the cosolvent and CDs + CB liposomes in PBS (pH = 7.4).

(a) CLSM images of HepG2 cells incubated with CDs liposomes (concentration of CDs: 40 μg mL−1) for 24 h and LysoTracker (Green) at 75 nM for 30 min. The scale bar corresponds to 20 μm; (b) Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of CDs liposomes against HepG2 cells after 24 h incubation; (c) Cellular cytotoxicity assessment of CB and CDs + CB liposomes against HepG2 cells. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a–c) In vivo PL images of nude mice treated with intratumor injection of CDs (the left) and CDs + CB liposomes (the right) 0.25 h, 1 h and 2 h post injection, respectively; (d) Ex vivo images of mice tissues 24 h post tail intravenous injection of CDs (the left) and CDs + CB liposomes (the right). The color bars represent the PL intensity.

(a) Tumor growth curves; (b) body weight; (c) H&E stained tissue sections from heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney of the mouse of different groups after treatments at 8 days. Scale bar corresponds to 100 μm. Student’s t-test was performed between saline group and CDs + CB liposomes group (*P < 0.05).

head.ce:title: Near-infrared fluorescent carbon dots encapsulated liposomes as multifunctional nano-carrier and tracer of the anticancer agent cinobufagin in vivo and in vitro

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Sciences
CAS Research/Education Center for Excellence in Molecular Sciences
Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Living Biosystems
Institute of Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Integrating the optical properties of near-infrared fluorescent carbon dots into liposomes may construct a multifunctional nano-system with the potential as a drug carrier, tracer and efficacy intensifier of the anticancer agent. In this study, the liposomes loaded with hydrophilic near-infrared carbon dots as a nano-carrier and tracer of lipophilic anticancer agent cinobufagin were developed. Prepared liposomes were characterized by particle size, morphology and entrapment efficiency. The drug release behavior, the tracer function, the anticancer effect and the side effect were investigated in vitro and in vivo. It was observed that the photoluminescence emission of carbon dots could be strongly enhanced up to 5 times by nano-liposomes. Due to this property, the bio-imaging of CDs + CB liposomes in vitro and in vivo could be clearly obtained. Our results also showed that the CDs + CB liposomes could be uptaken by cells (the lysosomes targeted) and delivered to the tumor site, and undoubtedly, the CDs + CB liposomes demonstrated sustained drug release, enhanced anticancer efficacy and low side effects in vivo. With the assistance of imaging function of CDs, the CDs + CB liposomes can easily display the distribution of drugs, which is very helpful for drug development and may open a novel avenue for drug delivery.

ce:section.ce:para: Within the last decade, liposomes have become the most clinically established nano-scale systems that were used to deliver drug cargos for cancer therapy such as Doxil®, Caelyx®, Myocet® and DepoCyt [1,2]. Easy biodegradability, reduced toxicity, enhanced biocompatibility and improved efficacy comprise the outstanding properties of liposomes compared with other delivery systems [1]. Liposomes are spherical nanostructures consisting of a lipid bilayer surrounding an aqueous core. Consequently, both lipophilic and hydrophilic molecules can be incorporated into the liposomes for delivery, especially for those with low solubility and high toxicity [1,3]. Moreover, liposomes exhibit a passive targeting effect based on enhanced permeability and retention (EPR) effect, thereby realizing the tumor targeted therapy [4–7]. These properties drive the deep application of liposomes in drug delivery.

Recently, we found that cinobufagin (CB), an active ingredient isolated from toad venom, could interact compactly with cancer cells [8]. In addition, CB exhibited effective therapeutic activity against a broad spectrum of cancers, including human breast cancer [9], liver cancer [10], lung cancer [11,12], colorectal cancer [13], prostate cancer [14] and osteosarcoma [15,16]. However, the poor water solubility and strong cardiotoxicity of CB limited its anticancer activity in clinical applications. Herein, the application of liposomes as the drug delivery system may be helpful to overcome these problems.

In our previous work, two fluorophores were successfully encapsulated into liposomes for the sensing of intracellular pH values [17]. Based on this work, we infer that both therapeutic and imaging agents could be incorporated into liposomes, and the drug biodistribution and its accumulation at the target sites can be monitored noninvasively by bio-imaging tools in vitro and in vivo. As a newly emerging star in nanomaterials, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have drawn intensive interest since they were firstly reported in 2004 [18]. CDs have unique merits including non-blinking photoluminescence (PL) emission, high photostability, low cytotoxicity, excellent biocompatibility, eminent water solubility and easy functionalization [19,20]. Thus, CDs have significant potential for use in various fields such as bioimaging, drug delivery and optoelectronics [21–27]. Therefore, integrating the optical properties of CDs into liposomes may construct a multifunctional nano-system with the potential as a drug carrier, tracer and efficacy intensifier of the anticancer agent.

In this study, we examined the feasibility of near-infrared fluorescent CDs encapsulated liposomes as a multifunctional nano-carrier, tracer and efficacy intensifier of the anticancer agent cinobufagin in vivo and in vitro. Prepared liposomes were characterized by particle size, morphology, entrapment efficiency (EE), and drug release in vitro. The in vitro and in vivo bio-imaging and anti-tumor activities were examined using cancer cell lines and xenograft model of MCF-7 cell line. In addition, by entrapping CDs into liposomes, the PL intensity of CDs was strongly enhanced (5-fold). Consequently, this nano-medicine possessed multiple functions: i) tumor targeted drug delivery and imaging; ii) sustained drug release; iii) enhanced PL emission of CDs; iv) enhanced anti-cancer efficacy of CB. Cumulatively, our results demonstrated the utility of the CDs encapsulated liposomes as an effective nanomaterial for simultaneous cellular imaging, tracking, and delivery of drug cargos.

Cholesterol, DSPE-MPEG2000 and DPPC were purchased from A.V.T Pharmaceutical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 1-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-3,5-diphenylformazan (MTT) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). CB was purchased from Baoji Herbest Bio-Tech Co., Ltd (Baoji, China). LysoTracker Green DND-26 probe, HPLC grade methanol, acetonitrile and MS grade formic acid were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific (Pittsburgh, PA, USA). Ultrapure water was obtained from a Milli-Q water purification system (Millipore Corporation, USA). All other chemicals and reagents were from commercial sources unless otherwise described.

HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells were obtained from the national experimental cell resource sharing platform (Beijing, China). Dulbecco’s modified eagle medium, fetal bovine serum and trypsin were purchased from Invitrogen Gibco (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Female BALB/c nude mice were provided by Cisco North Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China).

The fluorescent intensity was detected on the F-4500 fluorescence spectroscopy (Hitachi, Japan) with the excited and emissive silt set as 10 nm. Ultraviolet-visible absorption spectra were measured using a TU-1900 UV–vis double-beam spectrometer (Purkinje General, China). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) data and zeta potential were recorded with Zetasizer Nano-ZS ZEN3600 spectrometer (Malvern, UK). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and cryo-TEM observations were performed by JEM-2011 F operating at 120 kV. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) photos were recorded with JEM-2100 F operating at 200 kV. Confocal laser scan microscopy (CLSM) observations were performed using a confocal laser scanning biological microscope FV1000-IX81 (Olympus, Japan). The absolute fluorescence quantum yields (QY) were measured by Edinburgh Instruments FLS980. The fluorescence lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS980 with a time correlated single photon containing method. A digital camera was used to capture the photographs of solutions.

CDs were prepared with modifications on our recently-developed procedures [28]. In brief, thiophene phenylpropionic acid (PPA) monomers were prepared initially via a suzuki reaction using thiophene-3-boronic acid and 3-(4-Bromophenyl) propionic acid under the catalysis of Pd(PPh3)4. Subsequently, PPA polymers were prepared via an oxidative polymerization of PPA monomers as follows: 4 equiv of FeCl3 was dissolved in 30 mL of dry CHCl3 under nitrogen, and then 1 equiv of PPA monomers dissolved in 20 mL of CHCl3 was added dropwise. The reaction mixture was stirred at room temperature for 2 days. The resulting precipitate was washed with methanol and dried under vacuum to obtain the desired PPA polymers. Finally, CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of PPA polymers, which was dissolved in alkaline solution, and then heated to 240 ℃ for 36 h. After cooling to room temperature, the resultant CDs solution was filtered through a Millipore 0.22 μm filter and then dispersed in water for further characterization and evaluation.

Herein, four types of liposomes were prepared: (1) liposomes without CDs or CB (blank liposomes), (2) liposomes loaded with CDs (CDs liposomes), (3) liposomes loaded with CB (CB liposomes) and (4) liposomes loaded with CDs and CB (CDs + CB liposomes). All the liposomes were prepared using the thin film hydration method [29]. In brief, 16 mg of DPPC, 4 mg of cholesterol, 2 mg of DSPE-MPEG2000 and 2 mg of CB were dissolved in 3 mL of chloroform. Then the organic solvent was evaporated off by means of rotary evaporation. Next, the dried lipid film was hydrated with 4 mL of CDs in phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and stirred at 50 ℃ for 1 h. The obtained solution was extruded through a 200 nm polycarbonate filter to form liposomes. The unencapsulated CDs and CB were removed via ultrafiltration (Amicon Ultra-4, MW = 10 kDa; Millipore, MA, USA) at 4000 g for 20 min. The as-prepared liposomes were then stored at 4 °C for further use. Blank liposomes, CDs liposomes and CB liposomes were also prepared by this method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of CB and CDs entrapment efficiency (EE)
ce:section.ce:para:
EE of CB and CDs were determined using the ultrafiltration method [30]. In brief, 1 mL of undialyzed CB + CDs liposomes was added into the centrifugal filter device (Amicon Ultra-4, MW = 10 kDa; Millipore, MA, USA) and centrifuged at 4000 g for 20 min. The ultrafiltrate was collected and subjected to fluorescent quantitation of CDs and UPLC-Qtrap-MS quantification of CB (to obtain free CDs concentration and free drug concentration [Cfree]). Total CDs concentration was known and total drug concentration ([Ctotal]) was derived as the ratio of the amount of drug added versus the total volume of the preparation. EE of CB and CDs were calculated according to the following formula:
E E
( % )
=
C t o t a l
- [ C f r e e ]
[ C t o t a l ]
× 100

The concentrations of CB in solutions and biological samples were quantified using MRM mode from an AB Sciex Qtrap® 4500 tandem MS (Foster, CA, USA) equipped with an ESI source connected to the UPLC system (I-class Acquity ultra performance liquid chromatography, Waters). Chromatographic separation was performed on a CSH C18 column (1.7 μm, 2.1 mm ID × 100 mm, Waters) maintained at 35 °C. The mobile phase was composed of water (0.1% formic acid, A) mixed in gradient mode with acetonitrile (B), at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The elution gradient was as follows: 0–5 min, 10% to 60% B; 5–10 min, 60% to 87% B; 10–12 min, 87% to 100% B; 12–15 min, 100% B. The injection volume was 3.0 μL and the sampler was set at 4 °C. Qtrap MS was operated in the positive ion mode with transition at m/z 443.2→365.2 for CB (collision energy = 25 V) and the other parameter settings have been described in our previous publication [8]. The peak areas of different concentrations of CB (8 ng/mL-4 μg/mL) analyzed by UPLC-Qtrap-MS were collected to establish standard curve and further calculate the concentrations of each analyte in real samples.

Drug release study was performed using a dialysis technique as described earlier [30]. In brief, 1 mL of CDs + CB liposome solution was placed in a dialysis bag (molecular weight cut-off of 3.5 kDa), followed by ligation with silk ties. PBS (pH = 7.4, 500 mL) maintained at 37 °C was used as the release medium under magnetic stirring. At each specified time point (0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24, 36 and 48 h), 0.2 mL of dialysate was withdrawn and replenished with the same volume of fresh release medium. The concentrations of CB were quantified by UPLC-Qtrap MS as described above. The release curve was plotted with cumulative drug release as the function of time. Similar experiment was carried out by use of free CB (the same concentration as that of CB in CDs + CB liposomes) that dissolved in methanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular uptake of CB and CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The intracellular uptake of CB was investigated by quantification of CB in the cells. Namely, HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells were seeded into cell culture flasks at a density of 3.0 × 106 cells/flask. After 24 h pre-incubation, cells were treated with CDs + CB liposomes or free CB for 5 h at the concentration of 500 ng/ml (CB equivalent). Then, cells were washed with PBS five times and harvested by trypsinization. After sonication in 200 μL of methanol for 5 min, cells were centrifuged at 12,000 g for 10 min to collect supernatant containing CB, which was quantified by UPLC-Qtrap-MS as described above. In addition, the intracellular uptake of CDs was also analyzed by the fluorescence of CDs using a fluorescence Microscope (Olympus, Japan). Namely, HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells were placed onto glass-bottom cell culture dishes (r = 15 mm) at a density of 5 × 104 cells per dish and were allowed to adhere at 37 °C for 24 h before treatment. Then, HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells were incubated with CDs or CDs liposomes at 37 °C for 0.5, 2, 5 and 24 h. After 0.5, 2 and 5 h incubation, cells were washed with PBS for 3 times and then rinsed with complete DMEM for subsequent CLSM imaging. After 24 h incubation, PBS was used to wash the cells three times and the lysosomes were counterstained by incubation with LysoTracker (Green) at 75 nM for 30 min before imaging. Then, they were washed three times with PBS again and observed for CLSM imaging (CDs and CDs liposomes: Ex:559 nm, Em: 575–675 nm; LysoTracker: Ex: 488 nm, Em: 510–520 nm).

The MTT-based assay was performed to compare the cytotoxicity of CB, CDs liposomes and CB + CDs liposomes in vitro. For cytotoxicity assay, MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells in logarithmic growth were plated in 96-well plates at a density of 6 × 103 cells per well in 100 μL of culture medium and were allowed to adhere for 24 h before treatment. Serial concentrations of each sample (CB, CDs liposomes and CB + CDs liposomes) were then added (100 μL per well). After treated for 24 h and 48 h, MTT solution (10 μL per well, 5 mg/mL solution) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C. The supernatant was then carefully removed, and 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide was added to each well. After the formazan crystals had dissolved completely, optical density at 570 nm was determined with a SpectraMax 190 microplate reader (Molecular Devices, Sunnyvale, CA, U.S.A). Dose-response curves were obtained, and the IC50 values were calculated by OriginPro 8.0.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo imaging and biodistribution of CDs + CB liposomes
ce:section.ce:para: Hereby, we declare that all the animal experiments were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines and the protocol was approved by the Ethics Committee of Institute of Chemistry Chinese Academy of Sciences. Firstly, a subcutaneous MCF-7 tumor was established by injecting a suspension of 2 × 107 MCF-7 cells in PBS (100 μL) into the buttock of each female BALB/c nude mouse (4-week-old, 16 g–18 g) and was allowed to grow for 10 days. When the tumors reached approximately 200 mm3, the mice were treated with intratumor and tail intravenous injections of 100 μL of CDs + CB liposomes. The nude mice injected with 100 μL of the same concentration of CDs were selected as controls. The mice PL imaging experiments were successfully performed at different time points (0.25, 1, 2, 4, 6 and 24 h) post intratumor injection using a PerkinElmer in vivo imaging system with 500 nm excitation light and a 640 nm emission. Prior to imaging, we firstly obtained pre-contrast data with excitation under the same excitation and emission wavelength ranges. Besides, to investigate the biodistribution of CDs + CB liposomes, the mice intravenously injected with CDs + CB liposomes were sacrificed by exsanguinations at 24 h post-injection, and the tumor and major organs (hearts, livers, spleens, lungs, and kidneys) were harvested for ex vivo PL imaging.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo study of the therapeutic efficacy
ce:section.ce:para: By tumor volume, the mice were randomly divided into three treatment groups: normal saline group (2.5 mL/kg, n = 6), CDs liposomes group (2.5 mL/kg, n = 6) and CDs + CB liposomes group (1 mg CB/kg, n = 6), on the basis of the solutions they were administered by intravenous injection through the vena caudalis every 2 days for 8 days. In the course of treatment, tumor volume (mm3) and body weight were used to evaluate the antitumor activity.

For histology analysis, major organs were treated according to the routine histological procedures. Briefly, they were embedded in paraffin. The paraffin embedded tumors and organs were cut at 4 μm thickness. Sections were subsequently stained with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) for histological examination.
Statistical analysis was performed with a two-tailed unpaired t-test and one-way ANOVA. All values were expressed as mean ± SD. P < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and characterization of CDs liposomes
ce:section.ce:para: To check the universality of liposomes induced PL enhancement of CDs, we further encapsulated another positively-charged CDs prepared from polythiophene derivatives reported in our previous reports [31–33] into liposomes. We noticed that the PL of the positively-charged CDs could also be enhanced as high as 4-fold (Fig. S3). These results demonstrated that the PL emission of CDs could be obviously improved after encapsulation into liposomes, which is a simple strategy to enhance the PL of CDs.

To understand the liposomes induced PL enhancement of CDs in more details, the QY, PL decay lifetime and corresponding radiative rate of CDs and CDs liposomes in solutions were investigated by steady state and transient state fluorescence spectrometer. The results showed that the QY of CDs increased up to 6 times and the PL lifetime of CDs decreased from 1.03 ns to 0.80 ns after encapsulation into liposomes (Fig. 3 ). Such a short lifetime indicates the possible mechanism of the radioactive recombination nature of excitations [34–36]. Further, the radiative rate was calculated according to the following equation: Kr =QY/τ, where Kr, QY and τ are the radiative rate, quantum yield and the PL decay lifetime, respectively. Owing to the change in QY and τ, the Kr constants of CDs increased from 6.21 × 106 S−1 to 49.25 × 106 S−1 after encapsulation into liposomes. It was reported that when the CDs were conjugated in the C-dot@C-dot nanoparticles, the radiative rate increased, leading to the enhanced PL emission [37]. Our experiment demonstrated the encapsulation of CDs into liposomes could also increase the radiative rate and enhance the PL emission of CDs, which was consistent with the previous report.

So far, the mechanism of CDs associated PL emission has not been clearly demonstrated. Owing to the numerous surface functional groups such as amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl, the PL properties of CDs can be strongly influenced through interaction with polymer nanoparticles [38]. As reported, several factors such as radical surface interactions, intramolecular rotational motions and other non-radiative decays usually impair the optoelectronic properties of CDs, severely leading to a low PL efficiency [39]. Herein, it is possible that the entrapment of CDs into nano-scale liposomes may effectively lower the vibration and rotation of CDs, decrease radical surface interactions, increase the radiative decay process and hence enhancing the PL intensity of CDs compared to the pure CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The preparation and characterization of CDs + CB liposomes
ce:section.ce:para: Further, CDs + CB liposomes were prepared and characterized. It was found that the CDs + CB liposomes featured an absorption peak at 440 nm and an emission peak at 640 nm, which was the same as that of CDs liposomes (Fig. 2b–c). In addition, the PL intensity of CDs + CB liposomes was also enhanced dramatically (4-fold enhancement, Fig. 2c). The cyro-TEM images revealed that the size of CDs + CB liposomes was also around 60–80 nm with well-defined spherical shape (Fig. S4a), which was consistent with the DLS analysis result (Fig. S4b). Particle size of nanoparticles plays key role in the drug release behavior and their fate in vivo. As reported, nanoparticles with particle size less than 200 nm tend to accumulate in the tumor sites by the EPR effect [4–7]. In the present study, the CDs and CB co-loaded liposomes maintained a size range below 200 nm and therefore would be expected to have a satisfactory drug accumulation at the tumor sites. The zeta potential of CDs + CB liposomes was −2.6 mV, indicating negative charges on the liposome surfaces, which may be due to the overall negative charge of DSPE-MPEG2000 and CDs. As we know, surface charge is a viable parameter to foretell the physical stability of a nanoparticle based colloidal system. Consequently, the repulsion between liposomes with the same sign of surface charge would avoid liposomes aggregation and provide extra stability of CDs + CB liposomes. As a matter of fact, the PL stability of CDs in CDs + CB liposomes was investigated during their storage at 4 °C. The results showed that the PL intensity of CDs in CDs + CB liposomes was almost unchanged in 30 days as shown in Fig. 2d. As the entrapment of CDs into liposomes could enhance the PL of CDs, the unchanged PL of CDs in CDs + CB liposomes demonstrated that the CDs + CB liposomes were stable during their storage at 4 °C in 30 days, and this long-term stability ensured their biological application.

The EE of CDs and CB in liposomes were investigated. The fluorescence results showed that the EE of CDs in CDs + CB liposomes was 88.7% ± 4.3. Further, quantification of CB was established by UPLC-Qtrap-MS (Fig. 4 a). The calibration curve of CB showed an excellent correlation between the peak area and concentration within the test ranges (Fig. 4b). The EE of CB in the CB liposomes and the CDs + CB liposomes were 95.5 ± 3.2 and 91.9 ± 3.5, respectively, demonstrating a high encapsulation percentage of CB in liposomes (Fig. 4c). Thus, due to the high drug loading capacity, the CDs based liposomes provided an ideal multifunctional delivery system for the treatment of cancers.

The in vitro drug release behavior of CB in liposomes was investigated in PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) at 37 °C. CB dissolved in methanol was used as a control, which has the same concentration as presented in liposomes. As depicted in Fig. 4d, the release rate of CB in liposomes was slower than that of the free CB solution. The free CB solution exhibited 90% release after 4 h, while the CDs + CB liposomes exhibited very slow release of 30% in the first 4 h, followed by the slow release of CB after 48 h under neutral condition, demonstrating sustained drug release of the CDs + CB liposomes. As a result, the sustained drug release of CB in the CDs + CB liposomes would maximize the therapeutic effectiveness of CB with minimal side-effects.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular uptake and in vitro cytotoxicity of CDs + CB liposomes
ce:section.ce:para: As expected, the cellular uptake of CB by MCF-7 and HepG2 cells was significantly improved in liposomes than pure CB (Fig. S5). The concentration of CB in liposomes was 1.5 times and 1.4 times than that of pure CB in MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells, respectively. The results suggested that liposomes could enhance the delivery of CB into cells.

Further, the cellular localization of CDs liposomes into MCF-7 and HepG2 cells was investigated at different time points. From the fluorescence images in Fig. S6a, we could observe a bright red circle on the membrane of HepG2 cells and MCF-7 cells after 0.5 h of incubation with CDs liposomes at 559 nm excitation, where no obvious fluorescence was observed in cells incubation with CDs, which indicated that CDs liposomes could penetrate into the cells easily in the first 0.5 h. The fluorescence reached equilibrium with 5 h of culturing. Moreover, under the same conditions, a weak PL signal was observed in the cells for the free CDs, demonstrating enhanced intracellular uptake of CDs in liposomes. After 24 h incubation, the merged images obtained from the overlay of the LysoTracker (Green) and CDs liposomes channels clearly showed that CDs liposomes could enter the cell lysosomes (Figs. 5 a and S6b). Taken together, these results indicated that liposomes could enhance the delivery of CB and CDs into cells than pure CB or CDs and the prepared CDs liposomes were cell lysosomes targeted.

Moreover, the in vitro cytotoxicity of CB, CDs liposomes and CB + CDs liposomes were measured by MTT assay using MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells. Nowadays, many research groups have devoted their work to the development of heavy metal-free nanotheranostics materials. Among them, CDs were considered to be advantageous over heavy metal quantum dots with respect to biocompatibility and low toxicity. In our present study, the liposomes containing 200 μg mL−1 CDs did not cause any significant cytotoxicity to MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells (Figs. 5b and S7a), suggesting that the drug-free liposomes were highly biocompatible. As shown in Figs. 5c and S7b, pure CB demonstrated a cytotoxic effect with a half maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) value of 15.19 ng/mL and 31.60 ng/mL against MCF-7 cells and HepG2 cells, respectively. In comparison, the liposomes appeared to significantly enhance the cytotoxic effect of CB with a smaller IC50 value of 8.62 ng/mL against MCF-7 cells and 19.90 ng/mL against HepG2 cells. The enhanced anticancer effect of liposomes may be attributed to increased cellular uptake and elevated drug accumulation within the cells (Fig. S5). The results indicated that liposomes could not only improve the PL emission of CDs, but also enhance the antitumor efficacy of CB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bio-imaging of CDs liposomes as tracer of CB in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of integrating the optical properties of CDs into liposomes as a drug tracer was investigated. Pure CDs or CDs + CB liposomes were injected into MCF-7 bearing nude mice intratumorally and then imaged by PerkinElmer in vivo imaging system under excitation at 500 nm. As can be seen in Figs. 6 a–c and S8, an obvious PL signal was detected at the injection site in 24 h after injection, and enhanced PL was observed in mice received intratumor injection with CDs + CB liposomes compared with pure CDs at different time points (0.25, 1, 2, 4, 6 and 24 h) post injection, demonstrating superior imaging property of CDs in liposomes.

Further, we studied the biodistribution of CDs + CB liposomes after intravenously injection of CDs + CB liposomes by monitoring the PL of CDs in the tumor and other major organs excised from the mice. As shown in Fig. 6d, the excised tumor tissue showed strong PL intensity, whereas the heart, spleen, lung and kidney showed very low signals. The CDs + CB liposomes mainly accumulated in the liver and the tumor tissues at 24 h post tail intravenous injection, similar to the in vivo behavior of many other previously reported nanoparticles [28,40]. These results demonstrated that the CDs + CB liposomes could specifically target the tumor site through the EPR effect after intravenous injection [41], which were very advantageous to realize imaging-guided drug delivery with high therapeutic efficiency and few toxic side effects. Moreover, enhanced PL of CDs + CB liposomes was also observed in the tumor area in comparison with the pure CDs even 24 h post tail intravenous injection (Fig. 6d), which could be attributable to the tumor passive targeting of EPR effects and reduced CDs degradation by liposomes encapsulation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The performance of anti-tumor activity of CDs + CB liposomes in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: The therapeutic outcomes were evaluated by monitoring the tumor volumes of the mice every other day for 8 days. CDs + CB liposomes showed significant antitumor activity in the MCF-7 tumor model. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, compared with the control group, CDs liposomes showed no obvious effect on the tumor in mice. When CB was administered into the mice, the tumor growth was significantly inhibited as revealed by the reduced tumor volume growth (about 30%) in comparison with that of control group.

In addition, the side effect was investigated by monitoring the body weight and H&E staining of the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney). In general, the loss of body weight was analyzed as an indicator for treatments-induced toxicity. As shown in Fig. 7b, no obvious body weight change was observed in the mice for all the groups, which indicated that the nano-system could effectively reduce the systemic toxicity of CB. We further investigated the potential toxicity of CDs + CB liposomes to the major organs in MCF-7 tumor bearing mice. On day 8, the major organs (heart, liver, spleen, lung and kidney) were removed and subjected to H&E staining. As expected, the organs of the mice treated with CDs + CB liposomes showed no apparent pathological abnormalities, suggesting no significant toxicity of multiple dosages of CDs + CB liposomes (Fig. 7c), which may be due to the sustained release behavior of CDs + CB liposomes as well as the reduction of drug exposure in normal tissues. The above results demonstrated that the present nano-liposomes based system held great potential in targeted drug delivery and cancer therapy with reduced systemic toxicity.

In summary, we successfully constructed a multifunctional liposomes based nano-carrier system for the delivery of hydrophobic therapeutic agent cinobufagin along with hydrophilic fluorescent carbon dots with integrated therapeutic and imaging functions. By comparison with the nanoparticles enhanced photoluminescence of carbon dots, liposomes played three crucial roles in this nano-carrier system: enhancing photoluminescence of carbon dots (5-fold), improving therapeutic efficacy of cinobufagin and achieving systemic delivery of carbon dots and cinobufagin. Moreover, the prepared liposomes displayed a unique combination of advantages, namely simple and versatile preparation, lysosomes targeted cellular imaging, tumor targeted delivery, enhanced photoluminescence emission/imaging, enhanced cellular uptake and improved anti-cancer activity, which will be highly interesting for applications in theranostics. We believe that the development of highly biocompatible and fluorescent drug delivery systems based on fluorescent carbon dots hold great promise for specific drug delivery with minimal side effects and toxicity in cancer therapy.
The authors report no conflicts of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China [grant numbers 21575146, 21874142, 21635008, 21621062 and 51472252].

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.11.041.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
